BIZ-OMICS · AQA A-Level Economics · Microeconomics

The Labour Market

How wages and employment are determined — and what happens when the labour market isn't perfectly competitive

1

The Derived Demand for Labour

Firms do not want labour for its own sake — they want labour because of what it produces. This makes the demand for labour a derived demand: it exists only because there is demand for the goods and services labour helps to make.

Derived Demand
Demand for a factor of production (such as labour) that arises not from any direct want for the factor itself, but from the demand for the final good or service that factor helps to produce.
1
Consumers demand a final good or service

For example, rising consumer demand for new-build housing.

2
Firms respond by expanding output of that good

Housebuilders increase the number of sites under construction to meet demand.

3
This directly raises demand for the labour used to produce it

Demand for bricklayers, electricians, and site managers rises, purely because demand for housing rose — not because of any change in the labour market itself.

★ UK EXAMPLE: Demand for Software Developers
The sharp rise in demand for UK software developers over the past decade did not stem from any change in developers' own preferences to work — it is a direct consequence of surging consumer and business demand for digital services, apps, and e-commerce platforms. As demand for these final products grew, so did the derived demand for the labour needed to build and maintain them.
Why This Matters Throughout This Chapter: because labour demand is derived, anything that shifts demand for a firm's output (companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide, Section 2) will also shift that firm's demand for labour in the same direction — this link, formalised through marginal productivity theory in Section 2, is the foundation of the entire labour demand side of this chapter.
2

The Marginal Productivity Theory of Labour Demand

The marginal productivity theory explains precisely how many workers a profit-maximising firm chooses to employ, and at what wage — by extending the marginal analysis used throughout this chapter's companion guides to the labour market specifically.

Marginal Physical Product of Labour (MPPL)
The additional output produced by employing one more unit of labour, holding all other factors of production constant — directly analogous to marginal product in the companion BIZ-OMICS Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 6.
Marginal Revenue Product of Labour (MRPL)
The additional revenue generated by employing one more unit of labour, calculated as the marginal physical product of labour multiplied by the marginal revenue from selling that extra output (MRPL = MPPL × MR).

Worked Example — Calculating MRPL

A bakery employs an additional baker who produces an extra 40 loaves per day (MPPL = 40). Each loaf sells for £3, and the bakery operates in a competitive market where price equals marginal revenue (MR = £3).

MRPL = MPPL × MR = 40 × £3 = £120 per day

Key point: the additional baker adds £120 of revenue per day to the bakery — this figure, not the number of loaves alone, is what the firm compares against the cost of employing that worker.

1
A profit-maximising firm employs workers up to the point where MRPL = wage rate

As long as an additional worker adds more to revenue (MRPL) than they cost in wages, employing them raises profit.

2
Beyond that point, employing further workers reduces profit

If the wage rate exceeds MRPL, the additional worker costs more than the extra revenue they generate.

3
The profit-maximising level of employment therefore occurs exactly where MRPL = W

This is the labour-market equivalent of the MC=MR profit-maximising rule from the companion BIZ-OMICS Market Structures guide, Section 3.

Why MRPL Falls as More Labour Is Employed: because of the law of diminishing returns (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 7), MPPL — and therefore MRPL — falls as more workers are added to a fixed quantity of capital in the short run. This is precisely why the labour demand curve, examined in Section 3, slopes downward.
3

The Labour Demand Curve & Shifts in Demand

The demand curve for labour shows the relationship between the wage rate and the number of workers a firm (or industry) is willing to employ — and, as established in Section 2, this curve is simply the MRPL curve.

Labour Demand Curve
A curve showing the number of workers employers are willing and able to employ at each wage rate, sloping downward because MRPL falls as employment rises (Section 2).
Fig 1: The labour demand curve. Because MRPL falls as more workers are employed (Section 2), the labour demand curve slopes downward — at wage W₁, firms are willing to employ L₁ workers, the quantity at which MRPL exactly equals the wage rate.
Cause of a Shift in Labour DemandExplanation
A change in demand for the final productBecause labour demand is derived (Section 1), a rise in demand for the good produced shifts labour demand in the same direction.
A change in labour productivityImproved worker productivity (e.g. through training or new technology) raises MPPL, and therefore MRPL, shifting the labour demand curve right.
A change in the price of the good producedA higher output price raises MR, and therefore MRPL (MRPL = MPPL × MR), shifting labour demand right.
A change in the price of substitute or complementary factorsA fall in the cost of capital equipment that can replace workers (e.g. automation) can shift labour demand left, as firms substitute capital for labour.
Skill-Biased Technological Change
A pattern of technological change that raises the productivity (and therefore MRPL) of skilled workers by more than it raises the productivity of low-skilled workers, shifting labour demand for the two groups in opposite directions simultaneously.
Fig 1b: Skill-biased technological change. The same technological advance can shift labour demand in opposite directions for different groups of workers. Panel (a): where new technology substitutes directly for low-skill tasks (e.g. automated checkouts, warehouse robotics), demand for low-skill labour shifts left (D₀→D₁), lowering both the wage (W₀→W₁) and employment (Q₀→Q₁) for that group. Panel (b): where the same technology is complementary to high-skill tasks (e.g. software engineers needed to build and maintain the new systems), demand for high-skill labour shifts right (D₀→D₁), raising both the wage and employment for that group.
Why This Matters for Wage Inequality: because a single wave of technological change can simultaneously reduce demand for low-skill labour and increase demand for high-skill labour, it directly widens the wage gap between the two groups — a pattern many economists point to as a significant contributor to rising UK and global wage inequality since the 1980s, alongside globalisation and the decline in trade union coverage (Section 12).
Exam Trap to Avoid: exactly as with the ordinary product demand and supply curves (companion Price Determination guide, Section 2), a change in the wage rate itself causes only a movement along the labour demand curve — only a change in one of the factors above causes the curve itself to shift.
4

The Elasticity of Demand for Labour

The elasticity of demand for labour measures how responsive the quantity of labour demanded is to a change in the wage rate — and, as with price elasticity of demand for goods, depends on a specific set of determinants.

DeterminantTends Towards Inelastic Labour DemandTends Towards Elastic Labour Demand
Ease of substituting capital for labourLabour is difficult to replace with machinery or automation (e.g. skilled surgeons).Labour can be readily substituted by capital (e.g. routine assembly-line tasks).
Labour costs as a proportion of total costsWages make up a small share of total costs, so a wage rise has little effect on overall costs.Wages make up a large share of total costs, so a wage rise significantly affects overall costs and output decisions.
Price elasticity of demand for the final goodDemand for the final product is price inelastic (companion Price Determination guide, Section 6), so firms can pass on higher labour costs without losing many sales, reducing the need to cut employment.Demand for the final product is price elastic, so firms cannot easily pass on higher labour costs, making employment more sensitive to wage changes.
Time periodThe short run, when firms have limited ability to substitute away from labour.The long run, when firms have more time to invest in labour-saving capital.
Fig 1c: The effect of labour demand and supply elasticity on wage outcomes. In the left-hand market, both D=MRP and S are relatively elastic (flatter), so equilibrium settles at a moderate wage (W1). In the right-hand market, both curves are relatively inelastic (steeper) — the same underlying market conditions instead produce a higher equilibrium wage (W2) at a similar quantity of labour, since a smaller change in quantity is needed to restore equilibrium after any disturbance. This illustrates why the determinants in the table above (ease of substitution, labour's share of costs, product demand elasticity) translate directly into different wage outcomes across occupations.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Skilled vs Routine Labour
Demand for highly skilled, hard-to-replace workers such as consultant surgeons tends to be relatively wage inelastic, since there are few if any substitutes for their specific expertise and hospitals cannot easily automate their role. By contrast, demand for workers performing routine, easily automated tasks — such as certain warehouse picking and packing roles — tends to be far more wage elastic, since firms can substitute towards robotics and automated systems relatively easily if wage costs rise.
5

The Supply of Labour to an Occupation

The number of workers willing to supply their labour to a particular occupation is influenced by both monetary and non-monetary considerations, not simply the wage rate on offer.

Monetary Considerations
Financial factors influencing the attractiveness of an occupation, principally the wage rate on offer, but also including pension contributions, bonuses, and other financial benefits.
Non-Monetary Considerations
Non-financial factors influencing the attractiveness of an occupation, including job satisfaction, job security, working conditions, status, holiday entitlement, and the intrinsic enjoyment (or unpleasantness) of the work itself.
Net Advantage
The overall attractiveness of an occupation once both monetary and non-monetary considerations are taken into account together — two occupations can offer very different wages yet have similar net advantage if non-monetary factors compensate for the wage difference.
Non-Monetary FactorEffect on Labour Supply to an Occupation
Job satisfactionOccupations offering high intrinsic satisfaction (e.g. creative or caring roles) can attract a larger supply of workers even at a comparatively lower wage.
Job dissatisfaction / unpleasant conditionsDangerous, unpleasant, or highly stressful occupations (e.g. deep-sea fishing, emergency medicine) may need to offer a wage premium to attract sufficient workers, sometimes called a "compensating wage differential."
Working conditionsFlexible working hours, remote-working options, or strong workplace safety standards can increase labour supply to an occupation independently of the wage on offer.
Status and prestigeOccupations carrying high social status may attract more applicants than the wage alone would suggest.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Nursing and Non-Monetary Motivation
Despite NHS nursing salaries often being lower than comparable private-sector roles requiring similar qualifications, nursing continues to attract a substantial supply of applicants — widely attributed to the strong non-monetary job satisfaction associated with caring work, illustrating how net advantage, rather than the wage rate alone, determines labour supply to an occupation.
6

The Labour Supply Curve & Shifts in Supply

The labour supply curve for a particular occupation shows the relationship between the wage rate and the number of workers willing to work in that occupation, and typically slopes upward.

Labour Supply Curve (to an Occupation)
A curve showing the number of workers willing and able to supply their labour to a particular occupation at each wage rate, generally sloping upward — higher wages attract more workers into that occupation from alternative uses of their time and skills.
Cause of a Shift in Labour Supply to an OccupationExplanation
Change in wages in competing occupationsA rise in wages for a similar, competing occupation can shift labour supply away from this occupation, as workers switch to the now relatively more attractive alternative.
Change in non-monetary factorsImproved working conditions, greater job security, or increased status can shift the supply curve right, at any given wage (Section 5).
Change in qualifications or training requiredA rise in the entry requirements for an occupation (e.g. additional mandatory qualifications) tends to reduce supply, shifting the curve left, since fewer workers can meet the new barrier to entry.
Net migrationImmigration of workers qualified for a particular occupation increases labour supply to that occupation, shifting the curve right.
Demographic changeChanges in the size of the working-age population, or in the number of people retiring from an occupation, shift labour supply accordingly.
The Backward-Bending Individual Labour Supply Curve — Boundary of What Is Required: some individual workers, once their wage rises beyond a certain point, may choose to work fewer hours rather than more, as the income effect of higher pay (valuing extra leisure time more than extra income) begins to outweigh the substitution effect (working more because each hour now pays more) — producing a supply curve for an individual's hours of work that bends backward at high wage levels. The specification is explicit that you are not required to understand the determinants of an individual's own labour supply decision, nor the backward-bending supply curve itself — this chapter therefore focuses only on the market/occupational supply curve (Section 5), which slopes upward throughout.
Fig 2b (beyond the specification — enrichment only): the backward-bending individual labour supply curve described above. From W₁ to W, the substitution effect dominates and the individual supplies more hours as the wage rises. Beyond W, the income effect dominates — at wage W₂, the individual chooses to supply fewer hours (X₁ is less than X₂), valuing additional leisure time more highly than the extra income a further hour of work would bring. Not required for the exam, but useful for understanding why this concept is excluded from the market-level analysis used throughout this chapter.
7

Wage Determination in a Perfectly Competitive Labour Market

Just as the companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide explains how the interaction of demand and supply sets the price of a good, the same logic determines the equilibrium wage rate in a perfectly competitive labour market.

Perfectly Competitive Labour Market
A labour market with many buyers (firms) and many sellers (workers) of labour, homogeneous labour within an occupation, perfect information about wages and job opportunities, and freedom of entry and exit — meaning no individual firm or worker can influence the wage rate.
Wage Taker
An individual firm or worker in a perfectly competitive labour market who must accept the ruling market wage rate, since they are too small, relative to the whole market, to influence it — directly analogous to a price taker in a perfectly competitive product market (companion Market Structures guide, Section 6).
Fig 2: Wage determination in a perfectly competitive labour market. The equilibrium wage (We) and level of employment (Le) occur where market labour demand (DL) and market labour supply (SL) intersect — the only wage at which the quantity of labour demanded equals the quantity of labour supplied.
1
At any wage above We, there is excess supply of labour

More workers want jobs at that wage than firms wish to employ, creating unemployment in that occupation and downward pressure on the wage.

2
At any wage below We, there is excess demand for labour

Firms want to hire more workers than are willing to work at that wage, creating unfilled vacancies and upward pressure on the wage.

3
Only at We do these pressures disappear

The individual firm, as a wage taker, then employs workers up to the point where MRPL = We (Section 2), determining the firm's own level of employment given the market wage.

8

Market Forces & Relative Wage Rates

The interaction of labour demand and supply (Sections 3 and 6) does not only determine wages in a single occupation — it also explains why relative wage rates differ so dramatically between different occupations across the economy.

Occupation PatternExplanation via Demand and Supply
High demand, low supply → high wagesOccupations requiring rare skills, extensive training, or natural talent (e.g. specialist surgeons, elite professional footballers) combine high MRPL-driven demand with a very limited supply of qualified workers, pushing equilibrium wages sharply upward.
Low demand, high supply → low wagesOccupations requiring few specific skills, with a very large pool of workers able to perform them (e.g. many entry-level retail or hospitality roles), tend to have lower equilibrium wages.
High demand, high supply → moderate wagesPopular but accessible professional occupations (e.g. general teaching roles) can have strong demand met by a correspondingly large supply of qualified applicants, producing wages in a moderate range.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Premier League Footballers vs Grassroots Coaches
Elite Premier League footballers command extraordinarily high wages because their MRPL is enormous (driving broadcasting revenue, sponsorship, and matchday income for their clubs) while the supply of individuals with their level of talent is vanishingly small. Grassroots football coaches, by contrast, generate far lower MRPL for their (often much smaller, non-commercial) clubs, while the supply of people qualified and willing to coach at that level is comparatively large — producing a vast wage gap explained entirely by demand and supply, without needing to invoke any judgement about the relative "worth" of the two roles.
All Real-World Markets Are Imperfectly Competitive to Some Degree: the perfectly competitive model in Section 7 provides the essential theoretical benchmark for understanding wage determination through market forces, but the specification is explicit that real-world labour markets always depart from this model to a greater or lesser extent — through monopsony power, trade unions, or imperfect information, examined in Sections 9–13. The perfectly competitive model remains the correct starting point precisely because it identifies what departs from it in any real labour market.
9

Imperfections in Real-World Labour Markets

Three specific sources of imperfection cause real-world labour markets to depart from the perfectly competitive model examined in Section 7: monopsony power, trade unions, and imperfect information.

Monopsony Power
Market power held by a buyer (here, an employer) rather than a seller — where a firm is a large enough employer, relative to a local or occupational labour market, that it can influence the wage rate it pays, rather than simply accepting a market-determined wage. Examined fully in Sections 10–11.
Trade Union
An organisation of workers that negotiates collectively with employers over pay and working conditions, potentially giving workers as a group a degree of market power they would lack as individual wage takers. Examined fully in Sections 12–13.
Imperfect Information (in the Labour Market)
A situation in which workers and/or employers lack full knowledge of relevant facts — workers may not know the true wage or conditions on offer elsewhere, and employers may not know the true productivity of a prospective employee before hiring them — preventing the labour market from allocating workers as efficiently as the perfectly competitive model assumes.
Source of ImperfectionEffect on the Labour Market
Monopsony powerWages and employment are pushed below the competitive level, as the employer restricts hiring to keep wage costs down (Section 11).
Trade unionsWages can be pushed above the level a purely competitive market would set, with effects on employment that depend on the underlying market structure (Section 13).
Imperfect informationWorkers may remain in lower-paid roles unaware of better opportunities elsewhere, and employers may misjudge candidates' true productivity, both preventing labour from moving to its most valued use.
Why This Section Sets Up the Rest of the Chapter: Sections 10–13 examine monopsony power and trade unions in detail, including how their interaction with each other can produce outcomes that differ substantially from either source of imperfection acting alone — a key theme in the strongest evaluative answers on this topic.
10

Monopsony Labour Markets

A monopsony labour market exists where a single firm (or a small number of firms acting together) is such a dominant employer, relative to the size of the local or occupational labour market, that it holds genuine power over the wage rate it pays — the buyer-side mirror image of monopoly power on the seller side (companion Market Structures guide, Section 22).

Monopsony
A market with a single dominant buyer — in the labour market context, a single dominant employer facing an upward-sloping labour supply curve for the entire market it employs from, rather than a horizontal, wage-taking supply curve.
Marginal Cost of Labour (MCL)
The additional cost to a monopsony employer of hiring one more worker — critically, because the employer faces an upward-sloping labour supply curve, hiring one more worker requires raising the wage paid to all existing workers as well, not just the new hire, making MCL rise faster than the average wage (ACL) as employment increases. This is the direct labour-market parallel to the relationship between MR and AR under imperfect competition (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 20).
Characteristic of a Perfectly Competitive EmployerCharacteristic of a Monopsony Employer
Faces a horizontal (perfectly elastic) labour supply curve — can hire as many workers as it wants at the going market wage.Faces the entire upward-sloping market labour supply curve itself — must raise the wage to attract additional workers.
Marginal cost of labour (MCL) equals the wage rate at every level of employment.MCL exceeds the wage rate (average cost of labour, ACL) at every level of employment beyond the first worker, since raising the wage to attract one more worker also raises the wage paid to all existing workers.
Is a wage taker (Section 7).Is a wage maker — able to set the wage it pays, subject to the labour supply curve it faces.
★ UK REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: Monopsony Employers
The NHS is frequently cited as holding substantial monopsony power in the market for UK-trained nurses and doctors, since it is by far the dominant employer of clinical staff in most parts of the country, giving it significant influence over pay scales relative to a smaller, more fragmented employer. Historically, single large employers dominating a "company town" — such as a major factory or mine that is the overwhelmingly dominant local employer — have exhibited classic monopsony characteristics, since workers in that local labour market have very few alternative local employers to switch to if they judge the wage on offer to be too low.

Fig. 3 (Section 11) shows the full monopsony diagram once the reasoning behind it has been established below.

11

How Monopsony Power Reduces Wages & Employment

A profit-maximising monopsony employer applies the same underlying logic as any profit-maximising firm — but because it faces a rising MCL curve (Section 10) rather than a horizontal one, the resulting wage and employment outcome differs sharply from the perfectly competitive benchmark (Section 7).

1
The monopsonist employs labour up to the point where MCL = MRPL

Exactly as any profit-maximising firm equates marginal cost with marginal benefit, the monopsonist keeps hiring additional workers only as long as the marginal cost of doing so (MCL) is less than the marginal revenue product they generate (MRPL = DL, Section 2).

2
The wage actually paid is read off the labour supply curve, not the MCL curve

Because MCL lies above the supply curve (Section 10), the wage the monopsonist needs to pay to attract that level of employment — found on SL — is lower than the MCL=MRPL employment level would suggest.

3
Both the wage and the level of employment end up below the perfectly competitive outcome

Compared with Fig. 2 (Section 7), the monopsony wage is lower than We and the level of employment is lower than Le — the monopsonist deliberately restricts hiring below the competitive level specifically to keep the wage it must pay to all workers lower.

Why This Is the Direct Labour-Market Equivalent of Monopoly: exactly as a monopolist in the product market restricts output below the competitive level to raise price and earn supernormal profit (companion Market Structures guide, Fig. 10), a monopsonist restricts employment below the competitive level specifically to lower the wage it pays. Both outcomes create a form of market failure relative to the competitive benchmark — in this case, workers are paid a wage below their true marginal revenue product, and society loses the output that the "missing" workers between the monopsony and competitive employment levels would otherwise have produced.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Historical Pay Restraint in the Public Sector
Critics of long-running UK public-sector pay restraint (for example, in nursing and teaching) have sometimes argued that the government's position as an overwhelmingly dominant employer in these occupations gives it monopsony-style power to hold pay growth below what a genuinely competitive labour market for these skills would otherwise generate — contributing to the recruitment and retention difficulties, and industrial action (Section 12), seen in these sectors in recent years.
Fig 3: The monopsony labour market. The monopsonist equates MCL with DL=MRPL to determine employment at Q2 (Step 1) — a lower quantity than the competitive employment level Q1, where S=AC would itself cross D=MRP. Reading up from Q2 to the S=AC curve (Step 2) gives the wage the monopsonist actually needs to pay to attract that many workers: W2, which is lower than the competitive wage W1. Both wage and employment (Step 3) therefore end up below the competitive outcome, exactly as the reasoning chain above predicts.
12

Trade Unions: Sources of Bargaining Power

A trade union negotiates collectively with employers on behalf of its members, potentially giving workers — who individually would simply be wage takers (Section 7) — genuine bargaining power over pay and conditions. How much power a union actually has varies considerably between different labour markets.

Trade Union
An organisation representing employees in a particular occupation or industry, which negotiates collectively with employers over pay, working conditions, and other employment terms on behalf of its members.
Collective Bargaining
The process by which a trade union negotiates pay and conditions with an employer (or group of employers) on behalf of all its members simultaneously, rather than each worker bargaining individually.
Factor Affecting Union Bargaining PowerExplanation
Union density (proportion of workers who are members)A union representing a very high proportion of workers in an industry can credibly threaten a strike that would seriously disrupt the employer, giving it stronger bargaining power than a union with low membership.
The elasticity of demand for labourWhere labour demand is relatively wage inelastic (Section 4) — for example, because the skill is difficult to substitute with capital — a union can negotiate higher wages with a smaller resulting fall in employment, making union action more attractive.
The ability of the employer to pass on higher costsWhere the employer sells into a market with relatively inelastic product demand (companion Price Determination guide, Section 6), higher wage costs can be passed on to consumers via higher prices, making the employer more willing to concede to union demands.
Legal frameworkUK trade union law (including ballot requirements before industrial action, and restrictions on some forms of secondary action) directly affects how easily unions can take effective, legally protected industrial action.
Substitutability of union labourWhere an employer can relatively easily hire non-union labour, use agency staff, or relocate production, union bargaining power is significantly weakened.
★ UK EXAMPLE: RMT and Rail Industrial Action
The RMT (National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers) has historically wielded significant bargaining power in UK rail disputes, reflecting both very high union density among key operational grades such as train drivers and signallers, and the difficulty rail operators face in substituting striking staff at short notice, given the specialist safety-critical training these roles require. This combination of high union density and low substitutability of labour has repeatedly translated into a credible strike threat and genuine negotiating leverage.
Declining UK Union Density Since the 1980s: UK trade union membership and collective bargaining coverage fell substantially from their peak around 1980, following a combination of legal reforms restricting union activity, the decline of heavily unionised traditional manufacturing industries, and the growth of more fragmented service-sector employment — a long-run structural shift directly relevant to the union bargaining power factors in the table above.
13

Trade Unions in Competitive & Monopsony Labour Markets

The effect of introducing a trade union on wages and employment depends critically on whether it is introduced into an already competitive labour market (Section 7), or into a monopsony labour market (Sections 10–11) — the two cases produce strikingly different predictions.

Bilateral Monopoly
A market situation combining a single dominant buyer (monopsony employer) with a single dominant seller (a trade union with substantial bargaining power) — the resulting wage is not uniquely determined by either side alone, but depends on the relative bargaining strength of both parties.

Case 1 — A Trade Union Enters a Previously Perfectly Competitive Labour Market

1
Before the union, the competitive wage is We and employment is Le (Section 7, Fig. 2)

Every firm is a wage taker, facing a horizontal labour supply curve at We.

2
The union negotiates a wage above We, effectively acting like a minimum wage (Section 14) for that occupation

Firms can no longer hire below the negotiated union wage.

3
Employment falls below Le, and unemployment (excess supply) appears in that occupation

At the higher union wage, more workers want jobs than firms are willing to employ — the standard prediction for a wage set above the competitive equilibrium in an otherwise competitive market, directly parallel to the effect of a national minimum wage examined in Section 14.

Case 2 — A Trade Union Enters a Monopsony Labour Market

1
Before the union, the monopsonist pays a wage below the competitive level and restricts employment below Le (Section 11)

The monopsonist is deliberately holding both wages and employment below what a competitive market would produce.

2
A union negotiating a wage between the monopsony wage and the competitive wage effectively removes the monopsonist's incentive to restrict hiring to control the wage

Because the union-negotiated wage is now fixed by the agreement rather than rising as the monopsonist hires more workers, MCL effectively becomes flat (equal to the negotiated wage) up to the point where the labour supply curve takes over again — removing the wedge between MCL and ACL that drove the restriction in Section 11.

3
It is possible for both the wage and the level of employment to rise simultaneously

Unlike Case 1, where a union wage above the competitive level necessarily reduces employment, a union wage introduced into a monopsony market can move both wages and employment closer to (or even to) the fully competitive levels — a genuinely counter-intuitive but well-established result.

Why This Distinction Is One of the Most Frequently Tested Points in This Topic: a very common exam error is to assume that any trade union wage rise must reduce employment. This is only guaranteed to be true in an otherwise competitive labour market (Case 1). Where the union is countering pre-existing monopsony power (Case 2), a wage rise can coincide with higher employment, up to the point where the negotiated wage reaches the fully competitive wage — beyond that point, further wage rises would once again reduce employment, exactly as in Case 1.
Case 1 Diagrammatically: a union wage set above We in an otherwise competitive market is mechanically identical to the minimum wage diagram in Fig. 5 (Section 14) — simply relabel the wage floor "union wage" in place of "NMW" and the same excess-supply-of-labour outcome applies.
Fig 4: Case 2 — a trade union entering a monopsony labour market. Left panel: the unconstrained monopsony outcome (Section 11), with wage W₁ and employment E₁, where MCL lies above D=MRPL at every quantity beyond the first worker. Right panel: once the union negotiates a fixed wage W₁, the monopsonist's MCL curve becomes flat (horizontal) at that wage up to the point where the original labour supply curve takes back over — removing the wedge between MCL and the wage that previously caused the monopsonist to restrict hiring. The result is a higher employment level E₁ at the same (or a higher) wage than the unconstrained monopsony outcome, exactly as the reasoning chain above predicts.
14

The National Minimum Wage: Effects on the Labour Market

A national minimum wage (NMW) is a legally binding wage floor, set by government, below which employers may not legally pay their workers — and its effect on employment depends critically on the underlying structure of the labour market it is applied to, echoing the union analysis in Section 13.

National Minimum Wage
A legal minimum hourly wage rate that employers must pay their employees, set by government, below which it is illegal to pay a worker regardless of any agreement between the employer and employee.
Fig 5: Real wage unemployment caused by a minimum wage set above the competitive equilibrium. At WMIN, quantity of labour supplied (QS) exceeds quantity demanded (QMIN) — the shaded triangle represents workers willing to work at WMIN who cannot find employment, known as real wage unemployment, illustrating the standard prediction of the competitive model when a wage floor is set above W1 (the equilibrium wage).
Fig 5b: A minimum wage introduced into a monopsony labour market. Without intervention, the monopsonist equates MCL (the steep line) with Labor Demand (MRPL) to set employment at Lm, paying only Wm — well below both the competitive wage Wc and the true value of that labour to the firm (Wu, read off the Demand curve at Lm). The gap between Wu and Wm is the wedge of "monopsonistic exploitation" described in Section 11. Once a minimum wage is set exactly at Wc, the monopsonist can hire any quantity up to Lc at that same flat wage — its effective MCL curve becomes horizontal at Wc up to Lc, only reverting to the original steep MCL curve beyond that point (the dashed portion). Because this flat effective MCL now meets Labor Demand exactly at (Lc, Wc), the minimum wage has restored the fully competitive wage and employment level simultaneously — both higher than the unconstrained monopsony outcome, exactly as Section 14's text predicts.
The Same Structural Point as Section 13: exactly as with a trade union wage (Section 13), the employment effect of a minimum wage depends on the underlying market structure. In an otherwise competitive labour market (Fig. 5), a binding minimum wage set above We is predicted to reduce employment and create unemployment. In a monopsony labour market, a minimum wage set appropriately between the monopsony wage and the competitive wage can, exactly as in Section 13's Case 2, raise both the wage and the level of employment simultaneously — a key argument used by economists who support minimum wage policy in markets suspected of monopsony power.
15

The History of the UK Minimum Wage & National Living Wage

The UK's minimum wage system has evolved considerably since its introduction, culminating in the current structure of the National Living Wage (NLW) alongside age-banded National Minimum Wage rates.

DateDevelopment
April 1999The UK's National Minimum Wage was introduced by the Labour government under Tony Blair, following recommendations of the newly created Low Pay Commission — the first legally binding minimum wage in UK history.
1999–2015The NMW was uprated periodically (typically annually) by the Low Pay Commission's recommendation, with different rates applying to different age bands, reflecting concerns that a uniform national rate might price younger, less experienced workers out of employment.
April 2016The Conservative government introduced the National Living Wage as a new, higher minimum wage rate specifically for workers aged 25 and over, set with an explicit long-term target of reaching a specified proportion of median UK earnings — a more ambitious wage floor than the standard NMW rates that continued to apply to younger workers.
April 2021The age threshold for the National Living Wage was lowered from 25 to 23, extending the higher NLW rate to a larger group of younger workers.
April 2024The age threshold for the National Living Wage was lowered further, to 21, continuing the long-run trend of extending the higher wage floor to progressively younger workers.
Why the Age-Banded Structure Matters for Exam Analysis: the continued existence of separate, lower NMW rates for workers below the NLW age threshold reflects the underlying economic concern examined in Fig. 5 — policymakers have historically judged that younger, typically less experienced workers face a labour market where their MRPL (Section 2) is often lower, making them more vulnerable to unemployment effects if a single, higher minimum wage rate were applied uniformly across all ages.
16

Advantages & Disadvantages of the National Minimum Wage

As with many of the interventions examined in this chapter, the National Minimum Wage presents a genuine mix of potential benefits and costs, with the balance depending heavily on the underlying structure of the specific labour market affected (Section 14).

Potential Advantages of a National Minimum Wage

  • Raises the income of the lowest-paid workers, directly addressing in-work poverty and reducing income inequality.
  • In a monopsony labour market, can raise both wages and employment simultaneously (Section 14), correcting an existing market failure rather than creating a new one.
  • May increase worker motivation and reduce staff turnover, potentially raising productivity (an efficiency-wage effect) that partially offsets the higher wage cost to employers.
  • Reduces reliance on in-work state benefits, potentially lowering the burden on public finances.

Potential Disadvantages of a National Minimum Wage

  • In an otherwise competitive labour market, a minimum wage set above the equilibrium wage is predicted to reduce employment and create unemployment (Fig. 5).
  • Firms facing higher labour costs may respond by raising prices, cutting non-wage benefits, reducing hours, or accelerating automation rather than simply paying more for the same quantity of labour.
  • Firms with the least ability to pass on higher costs (e.g. those facing elastic product demand, companion Price Determination guide, Section 6) are most likely to cut employment in response.
  • Setting the minimum wage significantly above the competitive wage in a genuinely competitive market risks disproportionately harming the very low-skilled or inexperienced workers it aims to help.
★ UK EVIDENCE: Employment Effects of the NMW/NLW
Since the NMW's introduction in 1999, extensive UK research (notably by the Low Pay Commission and independent academic studies) has generally found employment effects to be smaller than the simple competitive model in Fig. 5 alone would predict — widely interpreted as evidence that many low-paid UK labour markets exhibit at least some monopsony-like characteristics (Section 10), allowing wage rises to be absorbed with a smaller employment cost than a purely competitive model would suggest.
Exam Application: the strongest evaluative answers on the NMW explicitly consider which labour market structure (perfectly competitive vs monopsonistic) is most plausible for the specific occupation or industry in the question, since this single judgement almost entirely determines the predicted direction of the employment effect.
17

Wage Discrimination: Necessary Conditions

Wage discrimination occurs when workers who are equally productive are paid different wages for reasons unrelated to genuine differences in productivity — and, like price discrimination in the product market (companion Market Structures guide, Section 25), requires specific conditions to be present and to persist.

Wage Discrimination
A situation in which workers with identical productivity (MRPL, Section 2) receive different wages, based on a characteristic unrelated to that productivity, such as gender or ethnicity.
Necessary ConditionExplanation
The employer must have some wage-setting powerIn a genuinely perfectly competitive labour market (Section 7), any employer paying below MRPL to one group of otherwise identical workers would simply lose those workers to competing employers willing to pay the full competitive wage — wage discrimination is therefore easier to sustain where employers hold some monopsony power (Section 10) or where information is imperfect (Section 9).
The discriminated group must have relatively inelastic labour supply to the discriminating employerIf affected workers can easily move to non-discriminating employers, the discriminating employer would struggle to sustain lower wages — discrimination is more likely to persist where switching employers is costly or restricted, for example by limited job opportunities elsewhere, caring responsibilities, or geographic immobility.
Groups must be identifiableThe employer must be able to distinguish between groups (e.g. by gender or ethnicity) in order to apply a different wage or offer different opportunities to each group.
Two Economic Models of Discrimination: economists distinguish between taste-based discrimination, where an employer (or co-workers, or customers) has a genuine personal preference against employing or working with a particular group, even at a cost to profit; and statistical discrimination, where an employer, facing imperfect information about any individual worker's true productivity (Section 9), uses observable group characteristics as an imperfect proxy for expected productivity, systematically disadvantaging individuals from a group with a lower average (even if the individual's own true productivity is identical to the average of another group).
18

Gender, Ethnicity & Wage Discrimination in Practice

Real-world wage gaps between groups reflect a combination of genuine, non-discriminatory factors and residual wage discrimination as defined in Section 17 — separating the two is central to how economists and policymakers assess the true extent of discrimination.

Factor Contributing to an Observed Wage GapIs This Wage Discrimination? (Section 17)
Occupational segregation — groups concentrated in different, differently-paid occupationsNot directly wage discrimination as defined above, though the underlying reasons for segregation (e.g. unequal access to training, historic barriers to entry) may themselves reflect broader labour market discrimination.
Differences in average hours worked — e.g. more part-time working linked to caring responsibilitiesNot wage discrimination in the strict sense (Section 17), though it may reflect unequal distribution of unpaid caring responsibilities between groups in wider society.
Differences in experience/career breaksNot wage discrimination in the strict sense, if genuinely linked to differences in accumulated human capital and therefore MRPL.
Equal pay for identical roles, identical experience, identical hours — yet a persistent unexplained gapThis residual, unexplained component is the part most directly consistent with genuine wage discrimination as defined in Section 17.
Occupational Crowding Hypothesis
A theory explaining how discrimination can produce a wage gap between groups even without any employer ever paying two workers differently for the identical job — historic barriers preventing a group from entering certain higher-paying occupations "crowd" that group's labour supply into a narrower range of remaining occupations, artificially inflating supply (and depressing wages) in those crowded occupations, while artificially restricting supply (and inflating wages) in the occupations the group was excluded from.
Fig 6: The occupational crowding hypothesis illustrated. Suppose a group historically faced barriers to entering neurosurgery (e.g. exclusion from medical training, discriminatory hiring) despite many members of that group being equally capable of the role. This restricts SN (supply of labour to neurosurgery), pushing the equilibrium wage up to WN. The workers excluded from neurosurgery still need employment, so they are "crowded" into more accessible occupations such as cleaning, artificially swelling SC beyond what it would otherwise be and depressing the equilibrium wage there to WB. The resulting wage gap (WN − WB) exists not because any single employer pays the same job differently, but because discrimination has segmented the labour market itself into crowded, lower-paid occupations and restricted, higher-paid ones.
Why This Is a Distinct Mechanism From Section 17's Conditions: the crowding hypothesis explains a wage gap that can persist economy-wide, across entire occupations, rather than depending on any single employer holding wage-setting power over individual workers (Section 17's first condition). It instead operates through the labour supply side directly — restricting which occupations a group can realistically enter reduces that group's effective bargaining position economy-wide, producing exactly the kind of relatively inelastic, restricted labour supply described in Section 17's second condition, but at the scale of an entire occupation rather than a single firm.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Historic Occupational Crowding by Gender
Occupations such as nursing, primary school teaching, and cleaning have historically employed a very high proportion of women, while occupations such as surgery, engineering, and senior corporate management have historically employed a very high proportion of men — a pattern many labour economists attribute in part to historic barriers (formal and informal) that restricted women's entry into the latter group of occupations, crowding female labour supply into the former. Even as formal barriers to entry have fallen, the wage gap between traditionally "female-dominated" and "male-dominated" occupations documented in UK gender pay gap data (below) is consistent with a lingering crowding effect operating alongside any residual direct discrimination.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The BBC Gender Pay Dispute (2017)
In 2017, the BBC's first published list of its highest-paid on-air staff revealed that only around a third of the top earners were women, and that men were paid substantially more than women in broadly comparable presenting roles. The dispute became a high-profile UK case study in wage discrimination when China Editor Carrie Gracie resigned from her post in protest, arguing she had discovered male international editors in equivalent roles were paid significantly more for what she considered comparable work — the BBC later settled and increased her pay, and the episode substantially increased public and policy attention on UK gender pay transparency.
★ UK POLICY RESPONSE: Gender Pay Gap Reporting
Since April 2017, UK employers with 250 or more employees have been legally required to publish annual gender pay gap data, reporting the difference between average male and average female pay across their whole organisation. Because this measure captures the overall gap (including occupational segregation and hours differences, not only the narrower "equal pay for equal work" component from Section 17), it is a broader measure of gender labour market inequality than wage discrimination alone, but has significantly increased public and employer awareness of persistent gender pay differences across UK industries.
19

Evaluating Wage Discrimination

Wage discrimination has significant, largely negative consequences for the workers directly affected, but its wider effects on employers and the economy as a whole are also worth assessing explicitly.

Impact on Workers

  • Directly reduces the income of discriminated-against workers relative to their true productivity, worsening income inequality.
  • Can discourage investment in education and training by affected groups, if the expected wage return to that investment is artificially reduced by anticipated discrimination.
  • May contribute to occupational segregation over time, as workers avoid entering occupations or firms where they anticipate discriminatory treatment.

Impact on Employers & the Economy

  • A discriminating employer paying below MRPL for equally productive labour is, in principle, forgoing a source of relatively cheap, high-value labour that a non-discriminating rival could exploit — a genuine competitive disadvantage in a sufficiently competitive product market.
  • At an economy-wide level, systematic discrimination represents a misallocation of labour resources, since workers are not necessarily employed according to their true comparative productivity, reducing total output below its potential.
  • Reputational and legal risk (under the UK Equality Act 2010, which makes wage discrimination on protected characteristics including sex and race unlawful) creates a direct cost to employers found to be discriminating.
Why "The Market Should Compete Away Discrimination" Is Only a Partial Argument: economic theory (see Section 17's conditions) suggests that in a genuinely competitive labour market with perfect information, discriminating employers should eventually be undercut by non-discriminating rivals willing to hire underpaid, equally productive workers — yet real-world wage gaps have persisted for decades. This is precisely why the conditions in Section 17 (employer wage-setting power, inelastic labour supply of the discriminated group, and imperfect information) matter so much for a complete answer: they explain why the competitive market mechanism alone has not been sufficient to eliminate discrimination in practice, and therefore why policy intervention such as the Equality Act and gender pay gap reporting (Section 18) is often judged necessary.
20

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From Product Demand to the Wage Rate

Consumer demand rises for a final good or service (companion Price Determination guide, Section 2) → because labour demand is derived (Section 1), this raises firms' demand for the labour used to produce it → the MRPL of workers in that occupation rises (Section 2), shifting the labour demand curve right (Section 3) → in a perfectly competitive labour market, this raises the equilibrium wage and level of employment (Section 7) → the size of this wage rise depends on the elasticity of both labour demand and labour supply in that specific occupation (Section 4).

The Golden Chain: From Monopsony to the Case for a Minimum Wage

A single employer dominates a local or occupational labour market, giving it monopsony power (Section 10) → because the employer faces the whole upward-sloping labour supply curve, its marginal cost of labour (MCL) exceeds the wage it pays (Section 10) → the profit-maximising monopsonist therefore restricts employment below, and pays a wage below, the level a competitive market would produce (Section 11) → introducing either a trade union (Section 13) or a national minimum wage (Section 14) set appropriately between the monopsony and competitive wage can raise both the wage and the level of employment simultaneously → this is the central economic justification for minimum wage policy in labour markets suspected of monopsony power, and directly explains why UK employment effects of the NMW have often been smaller than a simple competitive model alone would predict (Section 16).

Key Exam Tips

1

Always begin labour demand questions by explaining why demand is derived (Section 1) before analysing any shift — this establishes the correct causal chain examiners are looking for.

2

For any MRPL calculation, always show MPPL and MR as separate steps before multiplying them together (Section 2) — method marks are awarded for each stage.

3

Remember the specification explicitly excludes the backward-bending individual labour supply curve (Section 6) — keep supply-side analysis at the market/occupational level, which slopes upward throughout.

4

For monopsony and trade union questions, always state explicitly which labour market structure you are analysing (perfectly competitive vs monopsony, Section 13) before predicting the employment effect of a wage change — this single judgement determines the direction of your answer.

5

For minimum wage evaluation questions, explicitly weigh the competitive-market prediction (job losses, Fig. 5) against the monopsony-market prediction (higher wages and employment) and use UK evidence (Section 16) to judge which is more plausible for the specific market in the question.

6

For wage discrimination questions, always check whether the scenario describes genuine productivity-related pay differences (not discrimination, Section 18) or a residual, unexplained gap between equally productive workers (genuine wage discrimination, Section 17) — conflating the two is a common and heavily penalised error.

BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | AQA A-Level Economics | The Labour Market
